title: Food and Drug Administration

infobox.value: * Food, Drug, and Insecticide Administration (July 1927 – July 1930) * Bureau of Chemistry, [USDA](/wiki/USDA "USDA") (July 1901 – July 1927) * Division of Chemistry, USDA (established 1862)
[Federal government of the United States](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States")

* [Marty Makary](/wiki/Marty_Makary "Marty Makary"), [Commissioner of Food and Drugs](/wiki/Commissioner_of_Food_and_Drugs "Commissioner of Food and Drugs")[[3]](#cite_note-3) * [Sara Brenner](/wiki/Sara_Brenner "Sara Brenner"), Principal Deputy Commissioner
[Department of Health and Human Services](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Health_and_Human_Services "United States Department of Health and Human Services")

* [Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research](/wiki/Center_for_Biologics_Evaluation_and_Research "Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research") * [Center for Devices and Radiological Health](/wiki/Center_for_Devices_and_Radiological_Health "Center for Devices and Radiological Health") * [Center for Drug Evaluation and Research](/wiki/Center_for_Drug_Evaluation_and_Research "Center for Drug Evaluation and Research") * [Center for Food Safety and Applied Nutrition](/wiki/Center_for_Food_Safety_and_Applied_Nutrition "Center for Food Safety and Applied Nutrition") * [Center for Tobacco Products](/wiki/Center_for_Tobacco_Products "Center for Tobacco Products") * [Center for Veterinary Medicine](/wiki/Center_for_Veterinary_Medicine "Center for Veterinary Medicine") * [National Center for Toxicological Research](/wiki/National_Center_for_Toxicological_Research "National Center for Toxicological Research") * [Office of Criminal Investigations](/wiki/Office_of_Criminal_Investigations "Office of Criminal Investigations") * [Office of Regulatory Affairs](/wiki/Office_of_Regulatory_Affairs "Office of Regulatory Affairs")
* [Pure Food and Drug Act](/wiki/Pure_Food_and_Drug_Act "Pure Food and Drug Act")

(**FDA**) is a [federal agency](/wiki/List_of_federal_agencies_in_the_United_States "List of federal agencies in the United States") of the [United States Department of Health and Human Services](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Health_and_Human_Services "United States Department of Health and Human Services") (HHS). The FDA is responsible for protecting and promoting [public health](/wiki/Public_health "Public health") through the control and supervision of [food safety](/wiki/Food_safety "Food safety"), [tobacco](/wiki/Tobacco "Tobacco") products, [caffeine](/wiki/Caffeine "Caffeine") products, [dietary supplements](/wiki/Dietary_supplement "Dietary supplement"), [prescription](/wiki/Prescription_drug "Prescription drug") and [over-the-counter](/wiki/Over-the-counter_drug "Over-the-counter drug") [pharmaceutical drugs](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_drug "Pharmaceutical drug") (medications), [vaccines](/wiki/Vaccine "Vaccine"), [biopharmaceuticals](/wiki/Biopharmaceutical "Biopharmaceutical"), [blood transfusions](/wiki/Blood_transfusion "Blood transfusion"), [medical devices](/wiki/Medical_device "Medical device"), [electromagnetic radiation](/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation") emitting devices (ERED), [cosmetics](/wiki/Cosmetics "Cosmetics"), [animal foods & feed](/wiki/Animal_feed "Animal feed"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) and [veterinary products](/wiki/Veterinary_medicine "Veterinary medicine").

The **Food and Drug Administration** (**FDA**) is a [federal agency](/wiki/List_of_federal_agencies_in_the_United_States "List of federal agencies in the United States") of the [United States Department of Health and Human Services](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Health_and_Human_Services "United States Department of Health and Human Services") (HHS). The FDA is responsible for protecting and promoting [public health](/wiki/Public_health "Public health") through the control and supervision of [food safety](/wiki/Food_safety "Food safety"), [tobacco](/wiki/Tobacco "Tobacco") products, [caffeine](/wiki/Caffeine "Caffeine") products, [dietary supplements](/wiki/Dietary_supplement "Dietary supplement"), [prescription](/wiki/Prescription_drug "Prescription drug") and [over-the-counter](/wiki/Over-the-counter_drug "Over-the-counter drug") [pharmaceutical drugs](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_drug "Pharmaceutical drug") (medications), [vaccines](/wiki/Vaccine "Vaccine"), [biopharmaceuticals](/wiki/Biopharmaceutical "Biopharmaceutical"), [blood transfusions](/wiki/Blood_transfusion "Blood transfusion"), [medical devices](/wiki/Medical_device "Medical device"), [electromagnetic radiation](/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation") emitting devices (ERED), [cosmetics](/wiki/Cosmetics "Cosmetics"), [animal foods & feed](/wiki/Animal_feed "Animal feed"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) and [veterinary products](/wiki/Veterinary_medicine "Veterinary medicine").

The FDA's primary focus is enforcing the [Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act](/wiki/Federal_Food,_Drug,_and_Cosmetic_Act "Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act") (FD&C). However, the agency also enforces other laws, notably Section 361 of the [Public Health Service Act](/wiki/Public_Health_Service_Act "Public Health Service Act") as well as associated regulations. Much of this regulatory-enforcement work is not directly related to food or drugs but involves other regulated products like [lasers](/wiki/Lasers "Lasers"), [cellular phones](/wiki/Cellular_phones "Cellular phones"), and [condoms](/wiki/Condoms "Condoms"). In addition, the FDA uses its authority to mitigate diseases in a variety of contexts, from household pets to [human sperm donated](/wiki/Sperm_donation "Sperm donation") for use in [assisted reproduction](/wiki/Assisted_reproduction "Assisted reproduction").

The FDA is led by the [commissioner of food and drugs](/wiki/Commissioner_of_Food_and_Drugs "Commissioner of Food and Drugs"), who is appointed by the president with the [advice and consent](/wiki/Advice_and_consent "Advice and consent") of the [Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"). The commissioner reports to the [secretary of health and human services](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Health_and_Human_Services "United States Secretary of Health and Human Services"). [Marty Makary](/wiki/Marty_Makary "Marty Makary") is the current commissioner.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The FDA's headquarters is located in the [White Oak](/wiki/White_Oak,_Maryland "White Oak, Maryland") area of [Silver Spring, Maryland](/wiki/Silver_Spring,_Maryland "Silver Spring, Maryland"), occupying the former [Naval Ordnance Laboratory](/wiki/Naval_Ordnance_Laboratory "Naval Ordnance Laboratory").[[6]](#cite_note-White_Oak-6) The agency has 223 field offices and 13 [laboratories](/wiki/Laboratories "Laboratories") located across the 50 states, the [United States Virgin Islands](/wiki/United_States_Virgin_Islands "United States Virgin Islands"), and [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico").[[7]](#cite_note-train-7) In 2008, the FDA began to post employees to foreign countries, including China, India, Costa Rica, Chile, Belgium, and the United Kingdom.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/FDA_Bldg_31_-_Exterior_%285161375422%29.jpg/250px-FDA_Bldg_31_-_Exterior_%285161375422%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:FDA_Bldg_31_-_Exterior_(5161375422).jpg)

FDA Building 31 houses the [Office of the Commissioner](/wiki/Commissioner_of_Food_and_Drugs "Commissioner of Food and Drugs") and the Office of Regulatory [Department of Health and Human Services](/wiki/Department_of_Health_and_Human_Services "Department of Health and Human Services").[[9]](#cite_note-9) The agency consists of fourteen centers and offices.[[note 1]](#cite_note-10)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

The FDA has regulatory oversight over a large array of products that affect the health and life of American citizens.[[10]](#cite_note-FDAHist-11) As a result, the FDA's powers and decisions are monitored by several governmental and non-governmental organizations. A $1.8 million 2006 [Institute of Medicine](/wiki/Institute_of_Medicine "Institute of Medicine") report on pharmaceutical regulation in the U.S. found major deficiencies in the current FDA system for ensuring the safety of drugs on the American market. Overall, the authors called for an increase in the [regulatory powers](/wiki/Regulations "Regulations"), funding, and independence of the FDA.[[11]](#cite_note-Henderson_2006-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)

## Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Location")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/FDA_Building_66_-_CDRH_%285160772175%29.jpg/250px-FDA_Building_66_-_CDRH_%285160772175%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:FDA_Building_66_-_CDRH_(5160772175).jpg)

FDA Building 66 houses the [Center for Devices and Radiological Health](/wiki/FDA_Center_for_Devices_and_Radiological_Health "FDA Center for Devices and Radiological Health").

### [Headquarters](/wiki/Headquarters "Headquarters")

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Headquarters")]

FDA headquarters facilities are currently located in [Montgomery County](/wiki/Montgomery_County,_Maryland "Montgomery County, Maryland") and [Prince George's County](/wiki/Prince_George%27s_County,_Maryland "Prince George's County, Maryland"), Maryland.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

### White Oak Federal Research Center

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: White Oak Federal Research Center")]

Since 1990, the FDA has had employees and facilities on 130 acres (53 hectares) of the White Oak Federal Research Center in the [White Oak](/wiki/White_Oak,_Maryland "White Oak, Maryland") area of [Silver Spring, Maryland](/wiki/Silver_Spring,_Maryland "Silver Spring, Maryland").[[6]](#cite_note-White_Oak-6)[[14]](#cite_note-coordinates-15) In 2001, the [General Services Administration](/wiki/General_Services_Administration "General Services Administration") (GSA) began new construction on the campus to consolidate the FDA's 25 existing operations in the [Washington metropolitan area](/wiki/Washington_metropolitan_area "Washington metropolitan area"), its headquarters in [Rockville](/wiki/Rockville,_Maryland "Rockville, Maryland"), and several fragmented office buildings. The first building, the Life Sciences Laboratory, was dedicated and opened with 104 employees in December 2003. As of December 2018,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit) the FDA campus has a population of 10,987 employees housed in approximately 3,800,000 square feet (350,000 square metres) of space, divided into ten offices and four laboratory buildings. The campus houses the [Office of the Commissioner](/wiki/Commissioner_of_the_Food_and_Drug_Administration "Commissioner of the Food and Drug Administration") (OC), the Office of Regulatory Affairs (ORA),  the [Center for Drug Evaluation and Research](/wiki/Center_for_Drug_Evaluation_and_Research "Center for Drug Evaluation and Research") (CDER), the [Center for Devices and Radiological Health](/wiki/FDA_Center_for_Devices_and_Radiological_Health "FDA Center for Devices and Radiological Health") (CDRH), the [Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research](/wiki/Center_for_Biologics_Evaluation_and_Research "Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research") (CBER) and offices for the [Center for Veterinary Medicine](/wiki/Center_for_Veterinary_Medicine "Center for Veterinary Medicine") (CVM).[[6]](#cite_note-White_Oak-6)

With the passing of the FDA Reauthorization Act of 2017, the FDA projects a 64% increase in employees to 18,000 over the next 15 years and wants to add approximately 1,600,000 square feet (150,000 square metres) of office and special use space to their existing facilities. The [National Capital Planning Commission](/wiki/National_Capital_Planning_Commission "National Capital Planning Commission") approved a new master plan for this expansion in December 2018,[[15]](#cite_note-16) and construction is expected to be completed by 2035, dependent on GSA appropriations.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

### Field locations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Field locations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Toxicology_Research_at_FDA_%28NCTR_Campus%29_%286023336064%29.jpg/500px-Toxicology_Research_at_FDA_%28NCTR_Campus%29_%286023336064%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toxicology_Research_at_FDA_(NCTR_Campus)_(6023336064).jpg)

The Arkansas Laboratory in [Jefferson, Arkansas](/wiki/Jefferson,_Jefferson_County,_Arkansas "Jefferson, Jefferson County, Arkansas") is the headquarters of the National Center for Toxicological Research.

#### Office of Regulatory Affairs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Office of Regulatory Affairs")]

The [Office of Regulatory Affairs](/wiki/Office_of_Regulatory_Affairs "Office of Regulatory Affairs") is considered the agency's "eyes and ears", conducting the vast majority of the FDA's work in the field.[[17]](#cite_note-18) Its employees, known as Consumer Safety Officers, or more commonly known simply as investigators, inspect production, warehousing facilities, investigate complaints, illnesses, or outbreaks, and review documentation in the case of medical devices, drugs, biological products, and other items where it may be difficult to conduct a physical examination or take a physical sample of the product. The Office of Regulatory Affairs is divided into five regions, which are further divided into 20 districts. The districts are based roughly on the geographic divisions of the [federal court system](/wiki/United_States_federal_court_system "United States federal court system"). Each district comprises a main district office and a number of Resident Posts, which are FDA remote offices that serve a particular geographic area. ORA also includes the Agency's network of regulatory laboratories, which analyze any physical samples taken. Though samples are usually food-related, some laboratories are equipped to analyze drugs, cosmetics, and radiation-emitting devices.

#### Office of Criminal Investigations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Office of Criminal Investigations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/US_Food_and_Drug_Administration_Office_20180930.jpg/250px-US_Food_and_Drug_Administration_Office_20180930.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Food_and_Drug_Administration_Office_20180930.jpg)

Jamaica, Queens, New York Regional Office - USFDA

The [Office of Criminal Investigations](/wiki/Office_of_Criminal_Investigations "Office of Criminal Investigations") was established in 1991 to investigate criminal cases. To do so, OCI employs approximately 200 special agents nationwide who, unlike ORA Investigators, are armed, have badges, and do not focus on technical aspects of the regulated industries. Rather, OCI agents pursue and develop cases when individuals and companies commit criminal actions, such as fraudulent claims or knowingly and willfully shipping known adulterated goods in interstate commerce. In many cases, OCI pursues cases involving violations of [Title 18 of the United States Code](/wiki/Title_18_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 18 of the United States Code") (e.g., conspiracy, false statements, wire fraud, mail fraud), in addition to prohibited acts as defined in Chapter III of the FD&C Act. OCI special agents often come from other criminal investigations backgrounds, and frequently work closely with the [Federal Bureau of Investigation](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation"), [assistant attorney general](/wiki/United_States_Assistant_Attorney_General "United States Assistant Attorney General"), and even [Interpol](/wiki/Interpol "Interpol"). OCI receives cases from a variety of sources—including ORA, local agencies, and the [FBI](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation"), and works with ORA Investigators to help develop the technical and science-based aspects of a case.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

#### Other locations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Other locations")]

The FDA has a number of field offices across the United States, in addition to international locations in China, India, Europe, the Middle East, and Latin America.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

## Scope and funding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Scope and funding")]

As of 2021, the FDA had responsibility for overseeing $2.7 trillion in food, medical, and tobacco products.[[20]](#cite_note-2021faqs-21) Some 54% of its budget derives from the federal government, and 46% is covered by industry user fees for FDA services.[[20]](#cite_note-2021faqs-21) For example, [pharmaceutical firms](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_industry "Pharmaceutical industry") pay fees to expedite drug reviews.[[20]](#cite_note-2021faqs-21)

According to [Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes"), pharmaceutical firms provide 75% of the FDA's drug review budget.[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)

## Regulatory programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Regulatory programs")]

### Emergency approvals (EUA)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Emergency approvals (EUA)")]

Emergency Use Authorization (EUA) is a mechanism that was created to facilitate the availability and use of medical countermeasures, including vaccines and personal protective equipment, during public health emergencies such as the Zika virus epidemic, the Ebola virus epidemic and the COVID-19 pandemic.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

### Regulations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Regulations")]

The programs for safety regulation vary widely by the type of product, its potential risks, and the regulatory powers granted to the agency. For example, the FDA regulates almost every facet of prescription drugs, including testing, manufacturing, labeling, advertising, marketing, efficacy, and safety—yet FDA regulation of cosmetics focuses primarily on labeling and safety. The FDA regulates most products with a set of published standards enforced by a modest number of facility inspections. Inspection observations are documented on [Form 483](/wiki/Form_483 "Form 483").[[24]](#cite_note-25)

In June 2018, the FDA released a statement regarding new guidelines to help food and drug manufacturers "implement protections against potential attacks on the U.S. food supply".[[25]](#cite_note-26) One of the guidelines includes the Intentional Adulteration (IA) rule, which requires strategies and procedures by the food industry to reduce the risk of compromise in facilities and processes that are significantly vulnerable.[[26]](#cite_note-27)[[27]](#cite_note-28)

The FDA also uses tactics of regulatory shaming,[[28]](#cite_note-29) mainly through online publication of non-compliance, warning letters, and "shaming lists." Regulation by shaming harnesses firms' sensitivity to reputational damage. For example, in 2018, the agency published an online "black list", in which it named dozens of branded drug companies that are supposedly using unlawful or unethical means to attempt to impede competition from [generic drug](/wiki/Generic_drug "Generic drug") companies.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

The FDA frequently works with other federal agencies, including the [Department of Agriculture](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Agriculture "United States Department of Agriculture"), the [Drug Enforcement Administration](/wiki/Drug_Enforcement_Administration "Drug Enforcement Administration"), [Customs and Border Protection](/wiki/U.S._Customs_and_Border_Protection "U.S. Customs and Border Protection"), and the [Consumer Product Safety Commission](/wiki/U.S._Consumer_Product_Safety_Commission "U.S. Consumer Product Safety Commission"). They also often work with local and state government agencies in performing regulatory inspections and enforcement actions.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

### Food and dietary supplements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Food and dietary supplements")]

Main article: [Regulation of food and dietary supplements by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/Regulation_of_food_and_dietary_supplements_by_the_U.S._Food_and_Drug_Administration "Regulation of food and dietary supplements by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration")

The regulation of food and dietary supplements by the Food and Drug Administration is governed by various statutes enacted by the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") and interpreted by the FDA. Pursuant to the [Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act](/wiki/Federal_Food,_Drug,_and_Cosmetic_Act "Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act") and accompanying legislation, the FDA has authority to oversee the quality of substances sold as food in the United States, and to monitor claims made in the [labeling](/wiki/Packaging_and_labeling "Packaging and labeling") of both the composition and the health benefits of foods.

The FDA subdivides substances that it regulates as food into various categories—including foods, [food additives](/wiki/Food_additive "Food additive"), added substances (human-made substances that are not intentionally introduced into food, but nevertheless end up in it), and [dietary supplements](/wiki/Dietary_supplements "Dietary supplements"). Dietary supplements or dietary ingredients include vitamins, minerals, herbs, [amino acids](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid"), and [enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme").[[31]](#cite_note-32) Specific standards the FDA exercises differ from one category to the next. Furthermore, legislation had granted the FDA a variety of means to address violations of standards for a given substance category.

Under the [Dietary Supplement Health and Education Act of 1994](/wiki/Dietary_Supplement_Health_and_Education_Act_of_1994 "Dietary Supplement Health and Education Act of 1994") (DSHEA), the FDA is responsible for ensuring that manufacturers and distributors of dietary supplements and dietary ingredients meet the current requirements. These manufacturers and distributors are not allowed to advertise their products in an adulterated way, and they are responsible for evaluating the safety and labeling of their product.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

The FDA has a "Dietary Supplement Ingredient Advisory List" that includes ingredients that sometimes appear on dietary supplements but need further evaluation.[[33]](#cite_note-34) An ingredient is added to this list when it is excluded from use in a dietary supplement, does not appear to be an approved food additive or recognized as safe, and/or is subjected to the requirement for pre-market notification without having a satisfied requirement.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

#### "FDA-Approved" vs. "FDA-Accepted in Food Processing"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: \"FDA-Approved\" vs. \"FDA-Accepted in Food Processing\"")]

The FDA does not approve applied coatings used in the [food processing industry](/wiki/Food_processing_industry "Food processing industry").[[35]](#cite_note-36) There is no review process to approve the composition of nonstick coatings; nor does the FDA inspect or test these materials. Through their governing of processes, however, the FDA does have a set of regulations that cover the formulation, manufacturing, and use of nonstick coatings. Hence, materials like [Polytetrafluoroethylene](/wiki/Polytetrafluoroethylene "Polytetrafluoroethylene") (Teflon) are not and cannot be considered as FDA Approved, but rather, they are a "FDA Compliant" or "FDA Acceptable".

### Medical countermeasures (MCMs)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Medical countermeasures (MCMs)")]

Further information: [Biosecurity](/wiki/Biosecurity "Biosecurity"), [Bioterrorism](/wiki/Bioterrorism "Bioterrorism"), and [Biosecurity in the United States](/wiki/Biosecurity_in_the_United_States "Biosecurity in the United States")

Medical countermeasures (MCMs) are products such as [biologics](/wiki/Biologics "Biologics") and [pharmaceutical drugs](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_drug "Pharmaceutical drug") that can protect from or treat the health effects of a chemical, biological, radiological, or nuclear (CBRN) attack. MCMs can also be used for prevention and diagnosis of symptoms associated with CBRN attacks or threats.[[36]](#cite_note-fdamcm-37) The FDA runs a program called the "FDA Medical Countermeasures Initiative" (MCMi), with programs funded by the federal government. It helps support "partner" agencies and organisations prepare for [public health emergencies](/wiki/Public_health_emergency_(United_States) "Public health emergency (United States)") that could require MCMs.[[36]](#cite_note-fdamcm-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38)

### Medications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Medications")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/FDA_Bldg_51_-_Main_Entrance_%285161374834%29.jpg/250px-FDA_Bldg_51_-_Main_Entrance_%285161374834%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:FDA_Bldg_51_-_Main_Entrance_(5161374834).jpg)

FDA Building 51 houses the [Center for Drug Evaluation and Research](/wiki/Center_for_Drug_Evaluation_and_Research "Center for Drug Evaluation and Research").

The [Center for Drug Evaluation and Research](/wiki/Center_for_Drug_Evaluation_and_Research "Center for Drug Evaluation and Research") uses different requirements for the three main drug product types: new drugs, generic drugs, and over-the-counter drugs. A drug is considered "new" if it is made by a different manufacturer, uses different [excipients](/wiki/Excipient "Excipient") or inactive ingredients, is used for a different purpose, or undergoes any substantial change. The most rigorous requirements apply to *new molecular entities*: drugs that are not based on existing medications.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

#### New medications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: New medications")]

New drugs receive extensive scrutiny before FDA approval in a process called a [new drug application](/wiki/New_drug_application "New drug application") (NDA).[[39]](#cite_note-40) During the [first Donald Trump presidency](/wiki/First_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "First presidency of Donald Trump"), the agency worked to make the drug-approval process go faster.[[40]](#cite_note-41)**: 10** Critics, however, argue that FDA standards are not sufficiently rigorous to prevent unsafe or ineffective drugs from getting approval.[[41]](#cite_note-42) New drugs are available only by prescription by default. A change to over-the-counter (OTC) status is a separate process, and the drug must be approved through an NDA first. A drug that is approved is said to be "safe and effective when used as directed". Drugs being produced by a new manufacturer can be approved through one of two faster processes: the [Abbreviated New Drug Application](/wiki/Abbreviated_New_Drug_Application "Abbreviated New Drug Application") (ANDA) or the [505(b)(2) regulatory pathway](/wiki/505(b)(2)_regulatory_pathway "505(b)(2) regulatory pathway") for complex generic or biosimilar medications.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

Very rare, limited exceptions to this multi-step process involving animal testing and controlled clinical trials can be granted out of compassionate use protocols. This was the case during the 2015 Ebola epidemic with the use, by prescription and authorization, of [ZMapp](/wiki/ZMapp "ZMapp") and other experimental treatments, and for new drugs that can be used to treat debilitating and/or very rare conditions for which no existing remedies or drugs are satisfactory, or where there has not been an advance in a long period of time. The studies are progressively longer, gradually adding more individuals as they progress from stage I to stage III, normally over a period of years, and normally involve drug companies, the government and its laboratories, and often medical schools and hospitals and clinics. However, any exceptions to the aforementioned process are subject to strict review and scrutiny and conditions, and are only given if a substantial amount of research and at least some preliminary human testing has shown that they are believed to be somewhat safe and possibly effective. (See FDA Special Protocol Assessment about Phase III trials.)

##### Advertising and promotion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Advertising and promotion")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Food_and_Drug_Administration "Special:EditPage/Food and Drug Administration") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(August 2019)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The FDA's Office of Prescription Drug Promotion (OPDP) has responsibilities that revolve around the review and regulation of prescription drug advertising and promotion. This is achieved through surveillance activities and the issuance of enforcement letters to pharmaceutical manufacturers.[[43]](#cite_note-44) Advertising and promotion for over-the-counter drugs is regulated by the [Federal Trade Commission](/wiki/Federal_Trade_Commission "Federal Trade Commission"). The FDA also implements regulatory oversight through engagement with third-party enforcer firms. It expects pharmaceutical companies to ensure that third-party suppliers and labs comply with the agency's health and safety guidelines.[[44]](#cite_note-45): 4

The drug advertising regulation[[45]](#cite_note-46) contains two broad requirements: (1) a company may advertise or promote a drug only for the specific indication or medical use for which it was approved by FDA. Also, an advertisement must contain a "fair balance" between the benefits and the risks (side effects) of a drug. The regulation of drug advertising in the U.S. is divided between the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and the Federal Trade Commission (FTC), based on whether the drug in question is a prescription drug or an over-the-counter (OTC) drug. The FDA oversees the advertising of prescription drugs, while the FTC regulates the advertising of OTC drugs.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

The term [off-label](/wiki/Off-label "Off-label") refers to the practice of prescribing a drug for a different purpose than what the FDA approved.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

Due to this approval requirement, manufacturers were prohibited from advertising [COVID-19 vaccines](/wiki/COVID-19_vaccine "COVID-19 vaccine") during the period in which they had only been approved under [Emergency Use Authorization](/wiki/Emergency_Use_Authorization "Emergency Use Authorization").[[48]](#cite_note-49)

##### Post-market safety surveillance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Post-market safety surveillance")]

After NDA approval, the sponsor must then review and report to the FDA every single patient adverse drug experience it learns of. They must report unexpected serious and fatal adverse drug events within 15 days, and other events on a quarterly basis.[[49]](#cite_note-50) The FDA also receives directly adverse drug event reports through its [MedWatch](/wiki/MedWatch "MedWatch") program.[[50]](#cite_note-51) These reports are called "spontaneous reports" because reporting by consumers and health professionals is voluntary.

While this remains the primary tool of [post-market safety surveillance](/wiki/Postmarketing_surveillance "Postmarketing surveillance"), FDA requirements for post-marketing risk management are increasing. As a condition of approval, a sponsor may be required to conduct additional [clinical trials](/wiki/Clinical_trials "Clinical trials"), called Phase IV trials. In some cases, the FDA requires risk management plans called [Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategies](/wiki/Risk_Evaluation_and_Mitigation_Strategies "Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategies") (REMS) for some drugs that require actions to be taken to ensure that the drug is used safely.[[51]](#cite_note-Nelson-52)[[52]](#cite_note-53) For example, [thalidomide](/wiki/Thalidomide "Thalidomide") can cause birth defects, but has uses that outweigh the risks if men and women taking the drugs do not conceive a child; a REMS program for thalidomide mandates an auditable process to ensure that people taking the drug take action to avoid pregnancy; many [opioid](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid") drugs have REMS programs to avoid addiction and diversion of drugs.[[51]](#cite_note-Nelson-52) The drug [isotretinoin](/wiki/Isotretinoin "Isotretinoin") has a REMS program called [iPLEDGE](/wiki/IPLEDGE "IPLEDGE").[[53]](#cite_note-54)

#### Generic drugs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Generic drugs")]

Generic drugs are chemical and therapeutic equivalents of [name-brand](/wiki/Name-brand "Name-brand") drugs, normally whose patents have expired.[[54]](#cite_note-TNBK-55) Approved generic drugs should have the same dosage, safety, effectiveness, strength, stability, and quality, as well as route of administration. In general, they are less expensive than their name brand counterparts, are manufactured and marketed by rival companies and, in the 1990s, accounted for about a third of all prescriptions written in the United States.[[54]](#cite_note-TNBK-55) For a pharmaceutical company to gain approval to produce a generic drug, the FDA requires scientific evidence that the generic drug is interchangeable with or therapeutically equivalent to the originally approved drug.[[55]](#cite_note-Therapeutic_Equivalance_of_Generic_Drugs-56) This is called an [Abbreviated New Drug Application](/wiki/Abbreviated_New_Drug_Application "Abbreviated New Drug Application") (ANDA).[[56]](#cite_note-57) 80% of prescription drugs sold in the United States are generic brands.[[57]](#cite_note-58)

##### Generic drug scandal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Generic drug scandal")]

In 1989, a major scandal erupted involving the procedures used by the FDA to approve generic drugs for sale to the public.[[54]](#cite_note-TNBK-55) Charges of corruption in generic drug approval first emerged in 1988 during the course of an extensive congressional investigation into the FDA. The oversight subcommittee of the [United States House Energy and Commerce Committee](/wiki/House_Energy_and_Commerce_Committee "House Energy and Commerce Committee") resulted from a complaint brought against the FDA by Mylan Laboratories Inc. of [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"). When its application to manufacture generics was subjected to repeated delays by the FDA, Mylan, convinced that it was being discriminated against, soon began its own private investigation of the agency in 1987. Mylan eventually filed suit against two former FDA employees and four drug-manufacturing companies, charging that corruption within the federal agency resulted in [racketeering](/wiki/Racketeering "Racketeering") and violations of [antitrust law](/wiki/Antitrust_law "Antitrust law"). "The order in which new generic drugs were approved was set by the FDA employees even before drug manufacturers submitted applications," and, according to Mylan, this illegal procedure was followed to give preferential treatment to certain companies. During the summer of 1989, three FDA officials (Charles Y. Chang, David J. Brancat, and Walter Kletch) pleaded guilty to criminal charges of accepting bribes from generic drug makers, and two companies ([Par Pharmaceutical](/wiki/Par_Pharmaceutical "Par Pharmaceutical") and its subsidiary Quad Pharmaceuticals)[[58]](#cite_note-59) pleaded guilty to giving bribes.

Furthermore, it was discovered that several manufacturers had falsified data submitted in seeking FDA authorization to market certain generic drugs. Vitarine Pharmaceuticals of New York, which sought approval of a generic version of the drug [Dyazide](/wiki/Dyazide "Dyazide"), a medication for high blood pressure, submitted Dyazide, rather than its generic version, for the FDA tests. In April 1989, the FDA investigated 11 manufacturers for irregularities; and later brought that number up to 13. Dozens of drugs were eventually suspended or recalled by manufacturers. In the early 1990s, the [U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission](/wiki/U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission") filed securities fraud charges against the Bolar Pharmaceutical Company, a major generic manufacturer based in Long Island, New York.[[54]](#cite_note-TNBK-55)

#### Over-the-counter drugs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Over-the-counter drugs")]

Over-the-counter (OTC) drugs are drugs like [aspirin](/wiki/Aspirin "Aspirin") that do not require a doctor's prescription.[[59]](#cite_note-60) The FDA has a list of approximately 800 such approved ingredients that are combined in various ways to create more than 100,000 OTC drug products. Many OTC drug ingredients had been previously approved prescription drugs, now deemed safe enough for use without a [medical practitioner](/wiki/Physician "Physician")'s supervision, like [ibuprofen](/wiki/Ibuprofen "Ibuprofen").[[60]](#cite_note-61)

#### Ebola treatment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Ebola treatment")]

In 2014, the FDA added an [Ebola](/wiki/Ebola "Ebola") treatment being developed by Canadian pharmaceutical company [Tekmira](/wiki/Tekmira_Pharmaceuticals "Tekmira Pharmaceuticals") to the [Fast Track program](/wiki/FDA_Fast_Track_Development_Program "FDA Fast Track Development Program"), but halted the phase 1 trials in July pending the receipt of more information about how the drug works. This was widely viewed as increasingly important in the face of a [major outbreak of the disease in West Africa](/wiki/2014_West_Africa_Ebola_outbreak "2014 West Africa Ebola outbreak") that began in late March 2014 and ended in June 2016.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

#### Coronavirus (COVID-19) testing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Coronavirus (COVID-19) testing")]

Main article: [COVID-19 testing](/wiki/COVID-19_testing "COVID-19 testing")

During the [coronavirus pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States"), FDA granted [emergency use authorization](/wiki/Emergency_use_authorization "Emergency use authorization") for [personal protective equipment](/wiki/Personal_protective_equipment "Personal protective equipment") (PPE), in vitro diagnostic equipment, [ventilators](/wiki/Ventilator "Ventilator") and other medical devices.[[62]](#cite_note-FDA-EUA-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64)[[64]](#cite_note-65)

On March 18, 2020, FDA inspectors postponed most foreign facility inspections and all domestic routine surveillance facility inspections.[[65]](#cite_note-FDA-COVID-19-inspections-66) In contrast, the [USDA's](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Agriculture "United States Department of Agriculture") [Food Safety and Inspection Service](/wiki/Food_Safety_and_Inspection_Service "Food Safety and Inspection Service") (FSIS) continued inspections of meatpacking plants, which resulted in 145 FSIS field employees who tested positive for COVID-19, and three who died.[[66]](#cite_note-CBS-COVID-67)

FDA scientist prepares blood donation samples for testing.

The [Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research](/wiki/Center_for_Biologics_Evaluation_and_Research "Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research") is the branch of the FDA responsible for ensuring the safety and efficacy of biological therapeutic agents.[[67]](#cite_note-68)[[68]](#cite_note-69) These include blood and blood products, vaccines, allergenics, cell and tissue-based products, and gene therapy products. New biologics are required to go through a premarket approval process called a [Biologics License Application](/wiki/Biologics_license_application "Biologics license application") (BLA), similar to that for drugs.[[69]](#cite_note-70)

The original authority for government regulation of biological products was established by the 1902 [Biologics Control Act](/wiki/Biologics_Control_Act "Biologics Control Act"), with additional authority established by the 1944 [Public Health Service Act](/wiki/Public_Health_Service_Act "Public Health Service Act"). Along with these Acts, the [Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act](/wiki/Federal_Food,_Drug,_and_Cosmetic_Act "Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act") applies to all biologic products, as well. Originally, the entity responsible for regulation of biological products resided under the [National Institutes of Health](/wiki/National_Institutes_of_Health "National Institutes of Health"); this authority was transferred to the FDA in 1972.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/FDA_Bldg_62_-_Exterior_%285161375340%29.jpg/250px-FDA_Bldg_62_-_Exterior_%285161375340%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:FDA_Bldg_62_-_Exterior_(5161375340).jpg)

FDA Building 62 houses the [Center for Devices and Radiological Health](/wiki/FDA_Center_for_Devices_and_Radiological_Health "FDA Center for Devices and Radiological Health").

The [Center for Devices and Radiological Health](/wiki/Center_for_Devices_and_Radiological_Health "Center for Devices and Radiological Health") (CDRH) is the branch of the FDA responsible for the premarket approval of all [medical devices](/wiki/Medical_device "Medical device"), as well as overseeing the manufacturing, performance, and safety of these devices.[[70]](#cite_note-71) The definition of a medical device is given in the FD&C Act, and it includes products from the simple [toothbrush](/wiki/Toothbrush "Toothbrush") to complex devices such as implantable [neurostimulators](/wiki/Neurostimulator "Neurostimulator"). CDRH also oversees the safety performance of non-medical devices that emit certain types of [electromagnetic radiation](/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation"). Examples of CDRH-regulated devices include [cellular phones](/wiki/Cellular_phones "Cellular phones"), [airport baggage screening equipment](/wiki/Airport_security "Airport security"), [television receivers](/wiki/Television "Television"), [microwave ovens](/wiki/Microwave_oven "Microwave oven"), [tanning booths](/wiki/Tanning_booth "Tanning booth"), and [laser products](/wiki/Laser "Laser").[[71]](#cite_note-72)

CDRH regulatory powers include the authority to require certain technical reports from the manufacturers or importers of regulated products, to require that radiation-emitting products meet mandatory safety performance standards, to declare regulated products defective, and to order the recall of defective or noncompliant products. CDRH also conducts limited amounts of direct product testing.

#### "FDA-Cleared" vs "FDA-Approved"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: \"FDA-Cleared\" vs \"FDA-Approved\"")]

Clearance requests are required for medical devices that prove they are "substantially equivalent" to the predicate devices already on the market. Approved requests are for items that are new or substantially different and need to demonstrate "safety and efficacy", for example they may be inspected for safety in case of new toxic hazards. Both aspects need to be proved or provided by the submitter to ensure proper procedures are followed.[[72]](#cite_note-73)

### Cosmetics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Cosmetics")]

|  |  |
