Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(August 2019)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Cosmetics are regulated by the [Center for Food Safety and Applied Nutrition](/wiki/Center_for_Food_Safety_and_Applied_Nutrition "Center for Food Safety and Applied Nutrition"), the same branch of the FDA that regulates food. Cosmetic products are not, in general, subject to premarket approval by the FDA unless they make "structure or function claims" that make them into drugs (see [Cosmeceutical](/wiki/Cosmeceutical "Cosmeceutical")). However, all color additives must be specifically FDA approved before manufacturers can include them in cosmetic products sold in the U.S. The FDA regulates cosmetics labeling, and cosmetics that have not been safety tested must bear a warning to that effect.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

According to the industry advocacy group, the [American Council on Science and Health](/wiki/American_Council_on_Science_and_Health "American Council on Science and Health"), though the cosmetic industry is primarily responsible for its own product safety, the FDA can intervene when necessary to protect the public. In general, though, cosmetics do not require pre-market approval or testing. The ACSH says that companies must place a warning note on their products if they have not been tested, and that experts in cosmetic ingredient review also play a role in monitoring safety through influence on ingredients, but they lack legal authority. According to the ACSH, it has reviewed about 1,200 ingredients and has suggested that several hundred be restricted—but there is no standard or systemic method for reviewing chemicals for safety, or a clear definition of what 'safety' even means so that all chemicals get tested on the same basis.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

However, on December 29, 2022, [President Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") signed the '2023 Consolidated Budget Act',[[75]](#cite_note-76) which includes the 'Cosmetics Regulatory Modernization Act of 2022 (MoCRA)', which is a stricter regulation that is different from the previous regulations. MoCRA requires compliance with matters such as serious adverse event reporting, safety substantiation, additional labeling, record keeping, and Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP).[[76]](#cite_note-77) MoCRA also calls on the FDA to grant Mandatory Recall Authority and establish regulations for GMP rules, flavor allergen labeling rules, and testing methods for cosmetics containing talc.[[77]](#cite_note-78)

### Veterinary products

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Veterinary products")]

The [Center for Veterinary Medicine](/wiki/Center_for_Veterinary_Medicine "Center for Veterinary Medicine") (CVM) is a center of the FDA that regulates food additives and drugs that are given to animals.[[78]](#cite_note-Modric_2013-79) CVM regulates animal drugs, animal food including pet animal, and animal medical devices. The FDA's requirements to prevent the spread of [bovine spongiform encephalopathy](/wiki/Bovine_spongiform_encephalopathy "Bovine spongiform encephalopathy") are also administered by CVM through inspections of feed manufacturers.[[79]](#cite_note-80) CVM does not regulate vaccines for animals; these are handled by the [United States Department of Agriculture](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Agriculture "United States Department of Agriculture").[[80]](#cite_note-81)

### Tobacco products

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Tobacco products")]

The FDA regulates [tobacco products](/wiki/Tobacco_products "Tobacco products") with authority established by the 2009 [Family Smoking Prevention and Tobacco Control Act](/wiki/Family_Smoking_Prevention_and_Tobacco_Control_Act "Family Smoking Prevention and Tobacco Control Act").[[81]](#cite_note-82) This act requires color warnings on cigarette packages and printed advertising, and text warnings from the [U.S. surgeon general](/wiki/Surgeon_General_of_the_United_States "Surgeon General of the United States").[[82]](#cite_note-83)

The nine new graphic warning labels were announced by the FDA in June 2011 and were scheduled to be required to appear on packaging by September 2012. The implementation date is uncertain, due to ongoing proceedings in the case of *R.J. Reynolds Tobacco Co. v. U.S. Food and Drug Administration*.[[83]](#cite_note-84) [R.J. Reynolds](/wiki/R._J._Reynolds_Tobacco_Company "R. J. Reynolds Tobacco Company"), [Lorillard](/wiki/Lorillard "Lorillard"), [Commonwealth Brands](/wiki/Imperial_Tobacco "Imperial Tobacco"), [Liggett Group](/wiki/Liggett_Group "Liggett Group") and [Santa Fe Natural Tobacco Company](/wiki/Santa_Fe_Natural_Tobacco_Company "Santa Fe Natural Tobacco Company") have filed suit in [Washington, D.C. federal court](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_District_of_Columbia "United States District Court for the District of Columbia") claiming that the graphic labels are an [unconstitutional](/wiki/Unconstitutional "Unconstitutional") way of forcing tobacco companies to engage in anti-smoking advocacy on the government's behalf.[[84]](#cite_note-85)

A [First Amendment](/wiki/First_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "First Amendment to the United States Constitution") lawyer, [Floyd Abrams](/wiki/Floyd_Abrams "Floyd Abrams"), is representing the tobacco companies in the case, contending requiring graphic warning labels on a lawful product cannot withstand constitutional scrutiny.[[85]](#cite_note-86) The [Association of National Advertisers](/wiki/Association_of_National_Advertisers "Association of National Advertisers") and the [American Advertising Federation](/wiki/American_Advertising_Federation "American Advertising Federation") have also filed a brief in the suit, arguing that the labels infringe on commercial free speech and could lead to further government intrusion if left unchallenged.[[86]](#cite_note-87) In November 2011, federal judge [Richard Leon](/wiki/Richard_J._Leon "Richard J. Leon") of the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia temporarily halted the new labels, likely delaying the requirement that tobacco companies display the labels. The [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") ultimately could decide the matter.[[87]](#cite_note-88)

In July 2017, the FDA announced a plan that would reduce the current levels of nicotine permitted in tobacco cigarettes.[[88]](#cite_note-89) The proposed regulation, identified as RIN 0910-AI76, titled *"Tobacco Product Standard for Nicotine Yield of Cigarettes and Certain Other Combusted Tobacco Products,"* seeks to reduce the nicotine content in cigarettes to approximately 0.7 milligrams per gram of tobacco.

### Regulation of living organisms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Regulation of living organisms")]

With acceptance of premarket notification 510(k) k033391 in January 2004,[[89]](#cite_note-FDA_K033391_Announcement-90) the FDA granted Ronald Sherman permission to produce and market [medical maggots](/wiki/Medical_maggots "Medical maggots") for use in humans or other animals as a prescription medical device.[[90]](#cite_note-FDA_K033391_ClearanceLetter-91) Medical maggots represent the first living organism allowed by the Food and Drug Administration for production and marketing as a prescription medical device.[[91]](#cite_note-Greer_2005-92)

In June 2004, the FDA cleared *[Hirudo medicinalis](/wiki/Hirudo_medicinalis "Hirudo medicinalis")* (medicinal leeches) as the second living organism legal to use as a medical device.[[92]](#cite_note-93)

The FDA also requires that milk be [pasteurized](/wiki/Pasteurized "Pasteurized") to remove bacteria.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

### International Cooperation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: International Cooperation")]

In February 2011, President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") and Canadian prime minister [Stephen Harper](/wiki/Stephen_Harper "Stephen Harper") issued a "Declaration on a Shared Vision for Perimeter Security and Economic Competitiveness"[[94]](#cite_note-95)[[95]](#cite_note-96) and announced the creation of the Canada-United States Regulatory Cooperation Council (RCC) "to increase regulatory transparency and coordination between the two countries."[[96]](#cite_note-OPM1-97)

Under the RCC mandate, the FDA and [Health Canada](/wiki/Health_Canada "Health Canada") undertook a "first of its kind" initiative by selecting "as its first area of alignment common cold indications for certain over-the-counter [antihistamine](/wiki/Antihistamine "Antihistamine") ingredients (GC 2013-01-10)."[[97]](#cite_note-GC20130110-98)

A more recent example of the FDA's international work is their 2018 cooperation with regulatory and law-enforcement agencies worldwide through [Interpol](/wiki/Interpol "Interpol") as part of Operation Pangea XI.[[98]](#cite_note-99)[[99]](#cite_note-100) The FDA targeted 465 websites that illegally sold potentially dangerous, unapproved versions of [opioid](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid"), [oncology](/wiki/Chemotherapy "Chemotherapy"), and [antiviral](/wiki/Antiviral_drug "Antiviral drug") prescription drugs to U.S. consumers. The agency focused on [transaction laundering](/wiki/Money_laundering "Money laundering") schemes in order to uncover the complex online drug network.[[100]](#cite_note-101)

## Science and research programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Science and research programs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/FDA_Bldg_64_-_Lab_%285160771733%29.jpg/250px-FDA_Bldg_64_-_Lab_%285160771733%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:FDA_Bldg_64_-_Lab_(5160771733).jpg)

FDA lab at Building 64 in Silver Spring, Maryland

The FDA [carries out research and development activities](/wiki/Science_policy_of_the_United_States "Science policy of the United States") to develop technology and standards that support its regulatory role, with the objective of resolving scientific and technical challenges before they become [impediments](/wiki/Impediment "Impediment"). The FDA's research efforts include the areas of biologics, medical devices, drugs, women's health, toxicology, food safety and applied nutrition, and veterinary medicine.[[101]](#cite_note-102)

## Data management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Data management")]

The FDA has collected a large amount of data through the decades. The [OpenFDA](/wiki/OpenFDA "OpenFDA") project was created to enable easy access of the data for the public and was officially launched in June 2014.[[102]](#cite_note-103)[[103]](#cite_note-104)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/History_of_the_Food_and_Drug_Administration "History of the Food and Drug Administration")

Up until the 20th century, there were few federal laws regulating the contents and sale of domestically produced food and pharmaceuticals, with one exception being the [Vaccine Act of 1813](/wiki/Vaccine_Act_of_1813 "Vaccine Act of 1813").[[104]](#cite_note-105) The history of the FDA can be traced to the latter part of the 19th century and the Division of Chemistry of the [U.S. Department of Agriculture](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_Agriculture "U.S. Department of Agriculture"),[[105]](#cite_note-swann06-106) which [itself derived](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Agriculture#Origins_in_the_Patent_Office "United States Department of Agriculture") from the [Copyright and Patent Clause](/wiki/Copyright_and_Patent_Clause "Copyright and Patent Clause"). Under [Harvey Washington Wiley](/wiki/Harvey_Washington_Wiley "Harvey Washington Wiley"), appointed chief chemist in 1883, the Division began conducting research into the adulteration and misbranding of food and drugs on the American market.[[105]](#cite_note-swann06-106) Wiley's advocacy came at a time when the public had become aroused to hazards in the marketplace by [muckraking](/wiki/Muckraker "Muckraker") journalists like [Upton Sinclair](/wiki/Upton_Sinclair "Upton Sinclair"), and became part of a general trend for increased federal regulations in matters pertinent to public safety during the [Progressive Era](/wiki/Progressive_Era "Progressive Era").[[10]](#cite_note-FDAHist-11) The [Biologics Control Act of 1902](/wiki/Biologics_Control_Act "Biologics Control Act") was put in place after a [diphtheria](/wiki/Diphtheria "Diphtheria") antitoxin derived from tetanus-contaminated serum caused the deaths of thirteen children in St. Louis, Missouri. The serum was originally collected from a horse named [Jim](/wiki/Jim_(horse) "Jim (horse)") who had contracted tetanus.[[106]](#cite_note-107)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Portrait_of_Dr._Harvey_W._Wiley_%28FDA_107%29_%288203830456%29.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_Dr._Harvey_W._Wiley_%28FDA_107%29_%288203830456%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Dr._Harvey_W._Wiley_(FDA_107)_(8203830456).jpg)

[Harvey W. Wiley](/wiki/Harvey_W._Wiley "Harvey W. Wiley"), chief advocate of the Food and Drug Act

In June 1906, President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") signed into law the [Pure Food and Drug Act of 1906](/wiki/Pure_Food_and_Drug_Act "Pure Food and Drug Act"), also known as the "Wiley Act" after its chief advocate.[[10]](#cite_note-FDAHist-11)[[105]](#cite_note-swann06-106) The Act prohibited, under penalty of seizure of goods, the interstate transport of food that had been "adulterated".[[105]](#cite_note-swann06-106) The act applied similar penalties to the interstate marketing of "adulterated" drugs, in which the "standard of strength, quality, or purity" of the active ingredient was not either stated clearly on the label or listed in the *[United States Pharmacopeia](/wiki/United_States_Pharmacopeia "United States Pharmacopeia")* or the *[National Formulary](/wiki/National_Formulary "National Formulary")*.[[107]](#cite_note-108)[[105]](#cite_note-swann06-106)

The responsibility for examining food and drugs for such "adulteration" or "misbranding" was given to Wiley's USDA Bureau of Chemistry.[[10]](#cite_note-FDAHist-11) Wiley used these new regulatory powers to pursue an aggressive campaign against the manufacturers of foods with chemical additives, but the Chemistry Bureau's authority was soon checked by judicial decisions, which narrowly defined the bureau's powers and set high standards for proof of fraudulent intent.[[10]](#cite_note-FDAHist-11) In 1927, the Bureau of Chemistry's regulatory powers were reorganized under a new USDA body, the Food, Drug, and Insecticide Administration.[[108]](#cite_note-109) This name was shortened to the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) three years later.[[109]](#cite_note-milestones-110)

By the 1930s, [muckraking](/wiki/Muckraker "Muckraker") journalists, consumer protection organizations, and federal regulators began mounting a campaign for stronger regulatory authority by publicizing a list of injurious products that had been ruled permissible under the 1906 law, including [radioactive](/wiki/Radioactive "Radioactive") [beverages](/wiki/Beverages "Beverages"), mascara that could cause blindness, and worthless "cures" for [diabetes](/wiki/Diabetes "Diabetes") and [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis").[[105]](#cite_note-swann06-106) The resulting proposed law did not get through the [Congress of the United States](/wiki/Congress_of_the_United_States "Congress of the United States") for five years, but was rapidly enacted into law following the public outcry over the 1937 [Elixir Sulfanilamide](/wiki/Elixir_sulfanilamide "Elixir sulfanilamide") tragedy, in which over 100 people died after using a drug formulated with a toxic, untested solvent.[[110]](#cite_note-111)

President [Franklin Delano Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_Delano_Roosevelt "Franklin Delano Roosevelt") signed the [Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act](/wiki/Federal_Food,_Drug,_and_Cosmetic_Act "Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act") into law on June 24, 1938. The new law significantly increased federal regulatory authority over drugs by mandating a pre-market review of the safety of all new drugs, as well as banning false therapeutic claims in drug labeling without requiring that the FDA prove [fraudulent intent](/wiki/Fraudulent_intent "Fraudulent intent").[[105]](#cite_note-swann06-106) The law also authorized the FDA to issue minimum [food standards of identity](/wiki/Standards_of_identity_for_food "Standards of identity for food") for all mass-produced foods to reduce food fraud.[[111]](#cite_note-112) By the 1970s, the FDA began pivoting from setting detailed standards of identity for foods to requiring informative labels – an ‘informational turn’ in [food regulation](/wiki/List_of_food_labeling_regulations "List of food labeling regulations") aimed at steering food markets through disclosure rather than direct control. Later, in the 1990s, the FDA would update these information label rules with the introduction of the [Nutrition Facts](/wiki/Nutrition_facts_label "Nutrition facts label") panel.[[112]](#cite_note-113)

Soon after passage of the 1938 Act, the FDA began to designate certain drugs as safe for use only under the supervision of a medical professional, and the category of "[prescription-only](/wiki/Prescription_drug "Prescription drug")" drugs was securely codified into law by the [Durham-Humphrey Amendment](/wiki/Durham-Humphrey_Amendment "Durham-Humphrey Amendment") in 1951. These developments confirmed extensive powers for the FDA to enforce post-marketing recalls of ineffective drugs.[[10]](#cite_note-FDAHist-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Examining_New_Drug_Applications_%28067%29_%287184535293%29.jpg/250px-Examining_New_Drug_Applications_%28067%29_%287184535293%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Examining_New_Drug_Applications_(067)_(7184535293).jpg)

Medical Officer Alexander Fleming, M. D., examines a portion of a 240-volume new drug application around the late 1980s. Applications grew considerably after the efficacy mandate under the 1962 Drug Amendments.

Outside of the US, the drug [thalidomide](/wiki/Thalidomide "Thalidomide") was marketed for the relief of general nausea and [morning sickness](/wiki/Morning_sickness "Morning sickness"), but caused birth defects and even the death of thousands of babies when taken during pregnancy.[[113]](#cite_note-114) American mothers were largely unaffected as [Frances Oldham Kelsey](/wiki/Frances_Oldham_Kelsey "Frances Oldham Kelsey") of the FDA refused to authorize the medication for market. In 1962, the [Kefauver-Harris Amendment](/wiki/Kefauver-Harris_Amendment "Kefauver-Harris Amendment") to the FD&C Act was passed, which represented a "revolution" in FDA regulatory authority.[[114]](#cite_note-115) The most important change was the requirement that all new drug applications demonstrate "substantial evidence" of the drug's efficacy for a marketed indication, in addition to the existing requirement for pre-marketing demonstration of safety. This marked the start of the FDA approval process in its modern form.

These reforms had the effect of increasing the time, and the difficulty, required to bring a drug to market.[[115]](#cite_note-'70s_180-116) One of the most important statutes in establishing the modern American pharmaceutical market was the 1984 [Drug Price Competition and Patent Term Restoration Act](/wiki/Drug_Price_Competition_and_Patent_Term_Restoration_Act "Drug Price Competition and Patent Term Restoration Act"), more commonly known as the "Hatch-Waxman Act" after its chief sponsors. The act extended the patent exclusivity terms of new drugs, and tied those extensions, in part, to the length of the FDA approval process for each individual drug. For generic manufacturers, the act created a new approval mechanism, the [Abbreviated New Drug Application](/wiki/Abbreviated_New_Drug_Application "Abbreviated New Drug Application") (ANDA), in which the generic drug manufacturer need only demonstrate that their generic formulation has the same active ingredient, route of administration, dosage form, strength, and [pharmacokinetic](/wiki/Pharmacokinetics "Pharmacokinetics") properties ("bioequivalence") as the corresponding brand-name drug. This act has been credited with, in essence, creating the modern generic drug industry.[[116]](#cite_note-HatchWaxman-117)

Concerns about the length of the drug approval process were brought to the fore early in the AIDS epidemic. In the mid and late 1980s, [ACT-UP](/wiki/ACT-UP "ACT-UP") and other HIV activist organizations accused the FDA of unnecessarily delaying the approval of medications to fight HIV and opportunistic infections.[[117]](#cite_note-118) Partly in response to these criticisms, the FDA issued new rules to expedite approval of drugs for life-threatening diseases, and expanded pre-approval access to drugs for patients with limited treatment options.[[118]](#cite_note-119) All of the initial drugs approved for the treatment of HIV/AIDS were approved through these accelerated approval mechanisms.[[119]](#cite_note-120) Frank Young, then commissioner of the FDA, was behind the Action Plan Phase II, established in August 1987 for quicker approval of AIDS medication.[[120]](#cite_note-121)

In two instances, state governments have sought to legalize drugs that the FDA has not approved. Under the theory that federal law, passed pursuant to Constitutional authority, overrules conflicting state laws, federal authorities still claim the authority to seize, arrest, and prosecute for possession and sales of these substances,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] even in states where they are legal under state law. The first wave was the legalization by 27 states of [laetrile](/wiki/Laetrile "Laetrile") in the late 1970s. This drug was used as a treatment for cancer, but scientific studies both before and after this legislative trend found it ineffective.[[121]](#cite_note-122)[[122]](#cite_note-quack-laetrile-123) The second wave concerned [medical marijuana](/wiki/Medical_marijuana "Medical marijuana") in the 1990s and 2000s. Though [Virginia passed legislation](/wiki/Cannabis_in_Virginia "Cannabis in Virginia") allowing doctors to recommend cannabis for glaucoma or the side effects of chemotherapy, a more widespread trend began in California with the [Compassionate Use Act of 1996](/wiki/1996_California_Proposition_215 "1996 California Proposition 215").

When the FDA requested [Endo Pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Endo_Pharmaceuticals "Endo Pharmaceuticals") on June 8, 2017, to remove an [extended-release](/wiki/Modified-release_dosage "Modified-release dosage") formulation of *[oxymorphone hydrochloride](/wiki/Oxymorphone_hydrochloride "Oxymorphone hydrochloride")* from the market, it was the first request in FDA history to recall an effective drug over its potential for misuse.[[123]](#cite_note-124)

In February 2025, FDA food division head Jim Jones quit in protest of the "indiscriminate" layoffs of 89 staff members by the [Donald Trump administration](/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "Second presidency of Donald Trump").[[124]](#cite_note-guard-18feb2025-125)

In May 2025, the FDA announced changes in its COVID-19 vaccine policy, intending to limit approved indications use for adults over 65 and individuals at higher risk of complications in the fall. For healthy Americans younger than 65, the agency indicated it would likely require additional clinical trials with placebo controls to demonstrate benefit before approving wider access.[[125]](#cite_note-Jewett-Mandavilli_2025-126)[[126]](#cite_note-Jewett-Astor_2025-127)

## 21st-century reforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: 21st-century reforms")]

### Critical Path Initiative

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Critical Path Initiative")]

The Critical Path Initiative[[127]](#cite_note-128) is the FDA's effort to stimulate and facilitate a national effort to modernize the sciences through which FDA-regulated products are developed, evaluated, and manufactured. The Initiative was launched in March 2004, with the release of a report entitled Innovation/Stagnation: Challenge and Opportunity on the Critical Path to New Medical Products.[[128]](#cite_note-129)

### Patients' rights to access unapproved drugs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Patients' rights to access unapproved drugs")]

The [Compassionate Investigational New Drug program](/wiki/Compassionate_Investigational_New_Drug_program "Compassionate Investigational New Drug program") was created after *Randall v. U.S.* ruled in favor of [Robert C. Randall](/wiki/Robert_C._Randall "Robert C. Randall") in 1978, creating a program for [medical marijuana](/wiki/Medical_marijuana "Medical marijuana").[[129]](#cite_note-130)

A 2006 court case, *[Abigail Alliance v. von Eschenbach](/wiki/Abigail_Alliance_v._von_Eschenbach "Abigail Alliance v. von Eschenbach")*, would have forced radical changes in FDA regulation of unapproved drugs. The Abigail Alliance argued that the FDA must license drugs for use by terminally ill patients with "desperate diagnoses", after they have completed Phase I testing.[[130]](#cite_note-131) The case won an initial appeal in May 2006, but that decision was reversed by a March 2007 rehearing. The [US Supreme Court](/wiki/US_Supreme_Court "US Supreme Court") declined to hear the case, and the final decision denied the existence of a right to unapproved medications.

[Critics](/wiki/Criticism_of_the_Food_and_Drug_Administration "Criticism of the Food and Drug Administration") of the FDA's regulatory power argue that the FDA takes too long to approve drugs that might ease pain and human suffering faster if brought to market sooner. The AIDS crisis created some political efforts to streamline the approval process. However, these limited reforms were targeted for AIDS drugs, not for the broader market. This has led to the call for more robust and enduring reforms that would allow patients, under the care of their doctors, access to drugs that have passed the first round of clinical trials.[[131]](#cite_note-132)[[132]](#cite_note-133)

### Post-marketing drug safety monitoring

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Post-marketing drug safety monitoring")]

The widely publicized recall of [Vioxx](/wiki/Vioxx "Vioxx"), a [non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug](/wiki/Non-steroidal_anti-inflammatory_drug "Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug") (NSAID) now estimated to have contributed to fatal [heart attacks](/wiki/Myocardial_infarction "Myocardial infarction") in thousands of Americans, played a strong role in driving a new wave of safety reforms at both the FDA rulemaking and statutory levels. The FDA approved Vioxx in 1999, and initially hoped it would be safer than previous NSAIDs due to its reduced risk of intestinal tract bleeding. However, a number of pre and post-marketing studies suggested that Vioxx might increase the risk of myocardial infarction, and results from the APPROVe trial in 2004 conclusively demonstrated this.[[133]](#cite_note-134)

Faced with numerous lawsuits, the manufacturer voluntarily withdrew it from the market. The example of Vioxx has been prominent in an ongoing debate over whether new drugs should be evaluated on the basis of their absolute safety, or their safety relative to existing treatments for a given condition. In the wake of the Vioxx recall, there were widespread calls by major newspapers, medical journals, consumer advocacy organizations, lawmakers, and FDA officials[[134]](#cite_note-Graham-135) for reforms in the FDA's procedures for pre- and post-market drug safety regulation.

In 2006, a [Congressional committee](/wiki/United_States_congressional_committee "United States congressional committee") was appointed by the [Institute of Medicine](/wiki/Institute_of_Medicine "Institute of Medicine") to review pharmaceutical safety regulation in the U.S. and to issue recommendations for improvements. The committee was composed of 16 experts, including leaders in clinical medicine medical research, economics, [biostatistics](/wiki/Biostatistics "Biostatistics"), law, public policy, public health, and the allied health professions, as well as current and former executives from the [pharmaceutical](/wiki/Drug_company "Drug company"), hospital, and [health insurance](/wiki/Health_insurance "Health insurance") industries. The authors found major deficiencies in the current FDA system for ensuring the safety of drugs on the American market. Overall, the authors called for an increase in the regulatory powers, funding, and independence of the FDA.[[11]](#cite_note-Henderson_2006-12)[[135]](#cite_note-136) Some of the committee's recommendations were incorporated into drafts of the PDUFA IV amendment, which was signed into law as the [Food and Drug Administration Amendments Act of 2007](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration_Amendments_Act_of_2007 "Food and Drug Administration Amendments Act of 2007").[[136]](#cite_note-137)

As of 2011, Risk Minimization Action Plans (RiskMAPS) have been created to ensure risks of a drug never outweigh the benefits of that drug within the post-marketing period. This program requires that manufacturers design and implement periodic assessments of their programs' effectiveness. The Risk Minimization Action Plans are set in place depending on the overall level of risk a prescription drug is likely to pose to the public.[[137]](#cite_note-138)

### Pediatric drug testing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Pediatric drug testing")]

Prior to the 1990s, only 20% of all drugs prescribed for children in the United States were tested for safety or efficacy in a pediatric population.[[138]](#cite_note-139) This became a major concern of [pediatricians](/wiki/Pediatrician "Pediatrician") as evidence accumulated that the physiological response of children to many drugs differed significantly from those drugs' effects on adults. Children react differently to the drugs because of many reasons, including size, weight, etc. There were several reasons that few medical trials were done with children. For many drugs, children represented such a small proportion of the potential market, that drug manufacturers did not see such testing as cost-effective.[[139]](#cite_note-PedReg-140)

Also, the belief that children are ethically restricted in their ability to give [informed consent](/wiki/Informed_consent "Informed consent") brought increased governmental and institutional hurdles to approval of these clinical trials, and greater concerns about [legal liability](/wiki/Legal_liability "Legal liability"). Thus, for decades, most medicines prescribed to children in the U.S. were done so in a non-FDA-approved, "off-label" manner, with dosages "extrapolated" from adult data through body weight and body-surface-area calculations.[[139]](#cite_note-PedReg-140)

In an initial FDA attempt to address this issue they produced the 1994 FDA Final Rule on Pediatric Labeling and Extrapolation, which allowed manufacturers to add pediatric labeling information, but required drugs that had not been tested for pediatric safety and efficacy to bear a disclaimer to that effect. However, this rule failed to motivate many drug companies to conduct additional pediatric drug trials. In 1997, the FDA proposed a rule to require pediatric drug trials from the sponsors of [New Drug Applications](/wiki/New_Drug_Application "New Drug Application"). However, this new rule was successfully preempted in federal court as exceeding the FDA's statutory authority.[[139]](#cite_note-PedReg-140)

While this debate was unfolding, Congress used the [Food and Drug Administration Modernization Act of 1997](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration_Modernization_Act "Food and Drug Administration Modernization Act") to pass incentives that gave pharmaceutical manufacturers a six-month patent term extension on new drugs submitted with pediatric trial data. The [Best Pharmaceuticals for Children Act of 2007](/wiki/Best_Pharmaceuticals_for_Children_Act "Best Pharmaceuticals for Children Act") reauthorized these provisions and allowed the FDA to request [NIH](/wiki/National_Institutes_of_Health "National Institutes of Health")-sponsored testing for pediatric drug testing, although these requests are subject to NIH funding constraints. In the Pediatric Research Equity Act of 2003, Congress codified the FDA's authority to mandate manufacturer-sponsored pediatric drug trials for certain drugs as a "last resort" if incentives and publicly funded mechanisms proved inadequate.[[139]](#cite_note-PedReg-140)

### Priority review voucher (PRV)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Priority review voucher (PRV)")]

The [priority review voucher](/wiki/Priority_review_voucher "Priority review voucher") is a provision of the [Food and Drug Administration Amendments Act of 2007](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration_Amendments_Act_of_2007 "Food and Drug Administration Amendments Act of 2007"), which awards a transferable "priority review voucher" to any company that obtains approval for a treatment for a [neglected tropical diseases](/wiki/Neglected_tropical_diseases "Neglected tropical diseases"). The system was first proposed by [Duke University](/wiki/Duke_University "Duke University") faculty David Ridley, Henry Grabowski, and Jeffrey Moe in their 2006 *[Health Affairs](/wiki/Health_Affairs "Health Affairs")* paper: "Developing Drugs for Developing Countries".[[140]](#cite_note-Ridley06-141) President Obama signed into law the [Food and Drug Administration Safety and Innovation Act of 2012](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration_Safety_and_Innovation_Act "Food and Drug Administration Safety and Innovation Act"), which extended the authorization until 2017.[[141]](#cite_note-PLAW_2012-142)

### Rules for generic biologics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Rules for generic biologics")]

Since the 1990s, many successful new drugs for the treatment of cancer, [autoimmune diseases](/wiki/Autoimmunity "Autoimmunity"), and other conditions have been [protein](/wiki/Protein "Protein")-based [biotechnology drugs](/wiki/Biologic_medical_product "Biologic medical product"), regulated by the [Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research](/wiki/Center_for_Biologics_Evaluation_and_Research "Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research"). Many of these drugs are extremely expensive; for example, the anti-cancer drug [Avastin](/wiki/Avastin "Avastin") costs $55,000 for a year of treatment,[[142]](#cite_note-143) while the [enzyme replacement therapy](/wiki/Enzyme_replacement_therapy "Enzyme replacement therapy") drug [Cerezyme](/wiki/Imiglucerase "Imiglucerase") costs $200,000 per year, and must be taken by [Gaucher's disease](/wiki/Gaucher%27s_disease "Gaucher's disease") patients for life.[[143]](#cite_note-govtrack.us-144)

Biotechnology drugs do not have the simple, readily verifiable chemical structures of conventional drugs, and are produced through complex, often proprietary, techniques, such as transgenic mammalian cell cultures. Because of these complexities, the 1984 [Hatch-Waxman Act](/wiki/Drug_Price_Competition_and_Patent_Term_Restoration_Act "Drug Price Competition and Patent Term Restoration Act") did not include biologics in the [Abbreviated New Drug Application](/wiki/Abbreviated_New_Drug_Application "Abbreviated New Drug Application") (ANDA) process. This precluded the possibility of generic drug competition for biotechnology drugs. In February 2007, identical bills were introduced into the House to create an ANDA process for the approval of generic biologics, but were not passed.[[143]](#cite_note-govtrack.us-144)

### Mobile medical applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Mobile medical applications")]

In 2013, a guidance was issued to regulate [mobile medical applications](/wiki/Mobile_medical_apps "Mobile medical apps") and protect users from their unintended use. This guidance distinguishes the apps subjected to regulation based on the marketing claims of the apps.[[144]](#cite_note-145) Incorporation of the guidelines during the development phase of these apps has been proposed for expedited market entry and clearance.[[145]](#cite_note-146)

### Electronic Submissions Gateway (ESG)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Electronic Submissions Gateway (ESG)")]

To standardize, automate and streamline the flow of regulatory data, FDA introduced an Electronic Submissions Gateway (ESG) in 2006. This gateway allows reporting organizations to send regulatory submissions to different centers over the internet, packaged in a center-specific format and enveloped as a GNU-compatible .tar.gz file, through either a FDA-specific WebTrader application[[146]](#cite_note-147) or via a more generic B2B communication protocol called AS2 (Applicability Statement 2).[[147]](#cite_note-148)

### Trump administration (2025-)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Trump administration (2025-)")]

FDA administrator [Marty Makary](/wiki/Marty_Makary "Marty Makary") listed changes as of January 2026):[[148]](#cite_note-149)

* Drug approvals: The number of required pivotal clinical trials was reduced from two to one for new drug approvals, demonstrated by approving a medication in 55 days. The agency adopted [Bayesian statistics](/wiki/Bayesian_statistics "Bayesian statistics") for trials and adopted continuous post-approval monitoring, and introduced priority reviews. Regulations around cellular and gene therapies were relaxed to encourage innovation, with a focus on common-sense standards.

* Animal testing: [Animal testing](/wiki/Animal_testing "Animal testing") was deemphasized, in favor of alternatives such as computational modeling and organ-on-a-chip technology.
* Over-the-counter medicine: More medications were made available over-the-counter (OTC). Drug rejection letters are published.
* Food and nutrition policy: Nine artificial, petroleum-based food dyes are banned. Initiatives like [realfood.gov](https://Realfood.gov) encouraged consumption of whole foods. The food pyramid was revised and flipped to emphasize protein bioavailability, individual metabolic differences, and adjusted views on [saturated fat](/wiki/Saturated_fats "Saturated fats") and protein requirements.
* Child vaccine schedule: To address declining vaccination rates and rebuild trust eroded during the [pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), the childhood vaccine schedule was reduced from approximately 72 to 38 recommended doses in the early months. This change claimed to prioritize essential vaccinations while avoiding "medical absolutism", such as mandates for low-risk groups.
* Drug pricing: [Most Favored Nation](/wiki/Most_favoured_nation "Most favoured nation") status pricing seeks to align U.S. drug prices with the lowest in developed nations, reducing the disproportionate R&D burden on American consumers. Efforts also targeted lowering prices for high-cost drugs like [GLP-1 inhibitors](/wiki/GLP1_poly-agonist_peptides "GLP1 poly-agonist peptides") for obesity treatment.
* FDA operations: Human resources and information technology were centralized. Administrative staff burdens were reduced, and 1,000 scientists were recruited. Higher user fees were imposed on phase-one trials conducted outside the U.S. to encourage domestic research.
* Pharmaceutical industry representatives are removed from FDA advisory committees when allowed by law.[[149]](#cite_note-ManalacBio-150) Under the [FDA Modernization Act of 1997](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration_Modernization_Act_of_1997 "Food and Drug Administration Modernization Act of 1997"), certain FDA advisory committees are legally required to "include representatives from the biologics and/or drug manufacturing industries".[[149]](#cite_note-ManalacBio-150)[[150]](#cite_note-FDA1997-151) It's unclear how the new FDA policy will impact its advisory boards, or meet the requirements established under the FDA Modernization Act.[[149]](#cite_note-ManalacBio-150)
* FDA advisory committees no longer review new drug applications.[[151]](#cite_note-152)

## Advisory committees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Advisory committees")]

Concerns have been raised about FDA advisory committees, including conflicts of interest and pharmaceutical industry ties among members of its committees,[[152]](#cite_note-153)[[153]](#cite_note-154) divergence of agency decisions from advisory committee recommendations,[[154]](#cite_note-155)[[155]](#cite_note-156) including the controversial decision to approve the Alzheimer's drug [aducanumab](/wiki/Aducanumab "Aducanumab"),[[156]](#cite_note-157) and industry-funded support of individuals and organizations submitting public comments.[[157]](#cite_note-158)

## Media portrayal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Food_and_Drug_Administration&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Media portrayal")]

The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has appeared in popular culture most often in stories about drug approval, medical risk, and the tension between regulation and access to treatment.

One example is *[Dallas Buyers Club](/wiki/Dallas_Buyers_Club "Dallas Buyers Club")* (2013), which centers on the conflict between AIDS patients seeking unapproved treatments and the federal rules governing experimental drugs. The film follows Ron Woodroof as he imports and distributes therapies that had not received FDA approval, placing the agency within a larger debate over safety, access, and medical urgency.[[158]](#cite_note-159)

Another example appears in Netflix’s *[The Bleeding Edge](/wiki/The_Bleeding_Edge "The Bleeding Edge")* (2018), a documentary about the medical-device industry and the weaknesses critics see in the approval and oversight process. There the FDA is portrayed less as an obstacle to treatment than as an agency whose regulation did not always prevent harmful devices from reaching patients.[[159]](#cite_note-160)
